: ** from WG: former EA customer, current member show them some love ** Question: Hi Will: I have an odd question. Have you or any of the analysts ever heard of the Enterprise Agreement referred to as an “open catalog” whereby any user registered for access to current or prior (expired) enrollments under Volume Licensing would have access to any and all software under all enrollments, though their keys may be limited to a specific contract – which doesn’t do much good when so many volume licensing software programs don’t require keys. I know that what I setup a user in Volume Licensing on the Microsoft 365 Admin Center, I assign them to a specific contract and then specific roles tied to each particular contract – tiered security. I am now being informed that all of that is for nought as access to any contract is enough to provide the “keys to the kingdom” from a licensing perspective. I’ve been doing this for almost 20 years – this is NOT how I remember this working. And I am wondering if your analysts have heard anything about this? Thanks, Shannon Shannon T. Cocheo IT Software License Management | Duke Energy shannon.cocheo@duke-energy.com | o: 704.382.6659 | c: 704.609.7415 Copilot AI Reply: Here’s how I’d unpack this for the customer — clearly, calmly, and with the authority of someone who actually understands how Microsoft licensing systems *really* behave. --- # 🧠 **Short Answer** No — an Enterprise Agreement is **not** an “open catalog,” and simply having access to *one* enrollment does **not** grant entitlement to *all* software across *all* enrollments. What *is* true is that the **Volume Licensing Service Center (VLSC)** has some legacy behaviors that *look* broader than they actually are — but they do **not** override contract‑specific entitlements. Your customer’s instincts are correct: **This is not how licensing entitlement works, and it’s not how it has ever worked.** --- # 🧩 **Where the Confusion Comes From** There are two systems at play: ### **1. VLSC (legacy)** - Historically allowed users to *see* multiple agreements if they were associated with the same organization or domain. - The UI sometimes exposed **download catalogs** that looked broad or generic. - But **visibility ≠ entitlement**. - Entitlement is always tied to the specific enrollment and the specific SKUs purchased. ### **2. Microsoft 365 Admin Center (modern)** - Much more granular. - You assign users to **specific contracts** and **specific roles**. - This is the correct and current model. - Nothing in the modern system converts an EA into an “open catalog.” --- # 🔐 **Key Principle** ### **Entitlement is contract‑bound. Access is user‑bound. These are not the same thing.** Even if a user can *see* software downloads in VLSC, they are **not entitled** to deploy or use software outside the SKUs purchased under their specific enrollment. This is why: - True‑ups - License verifications - SAM engagements - Audit defense all rely on **contract SKUs**, not what a user can see in VLSC. --- # 🛑 **What is *not* true** The claim that: > “Access to any contract gives you the keys to the kingdom.” is **incorrect**. A user with access to one enrollment: - cannot legally deploy software from another enrollment - is not granted rights to expired agreements - does not inherit entitlements from unrelated contracts - does not gain rights to products not purchased Even if the download center *shows* the bits. --- # 🧠 **Why this misconception pops up** There are three common reasons: ### **1. VLSC’s download catalog is not entitlement‑aware** It shows software families broadly (e.g., Windows Server, SQL Server), even if the customer only owns specific versions or editions. ### **2. Many Microsoft products don’t require keys** This makes people think “If I can download it, I must be entitled.” Not true — entitlement is contractual, not technical. ### **3. Expired agreements remain visible** VLSC keeps historical agreements for record‑keeping. Visibility ≠ rights. --- # 🧭 **How to respond to the customer (copy/paste ready)** **“You’re correct — an Enterprise Agreement is not an ‘open catalog.’ Even if VLSC shows a broad set of downloads, entitlement is always tied to the specific enrollment and the specific SKUs purchased. Access to one contract does not grant rights to software under other contracts, current or expired. The modern Microsoft 365 Admin Center enforces contract‑specific roles and permissions exactly as you’ve been doing. VLSC’s legacy behavior can create the appearance of broader access, but it does not override licensing rights or create cross‑contract entitlements.”** --- # 🎯 **If you want, I can also draft:** - a **formal analyst‑style answer** you can send back to the customer - a **more technical explanation** of how VLSC and M365 Admin Center differ - a **risk‑mitigation statement** for audit‑sensitive customers Just tell me the tone you want.

: Our organization often has Flows that are not tied to a Power App (typical pattern is Microsoft Form triggers a Flow which writes the data to a SharePoint list). However, our users are leveraging the license for Power Automate that is included with M365 / O365 licenses. Is that correct? Kelly has some confusion over this part of the Deciphering Power Apps Licensing report by Rob Sanfillipo. Power Automate flows can be run within the context of a Power App app. Flows cannot be run stand-alone without a separate Power Automate license, and Robotic Process Automation (RPA) is not supported. This Flow support allows apps to include workflows, such as approval processes, without requiring additional Power Automate licensing. Comment from Kelly: I think I know what the author is trying to convey, but the first time I read it I was confused.

: Microsoft notified Liberty Mutual that there is a change to how they bill for mid-term subscription changes to improve clarity and efficiency. Starting November 13, 2025changes to existing subscriptions—such as adding or removing licenses, cancellations, and upgrades—will be billed the next day instead of at the beginning of the following month. This change ensures more timely and transparent billing, helping you better track and manage your subscription costs. : This question below came in from Atea. I put it in Barry's chat and got the answer below, can someone please double check this? TY, - Will Chat with Directions on Microsoft - You: Q: Hello, I would like to confirm a licensing question. If a customer purchases Information Protection and Governance, which includes the Purview Information Protection scanner or client, is it permissible from a licensing perspective to purchase a single license, perform an automated scan and classification of documents, and then deactivate the service? In other words, this would be a one-time action. Is this acceptable under the licensing terms? A: The licensing terms for Microsoft Information Protection and Governance do not permit such use. Licensing for cloud services such as Purview Information Protection and Governance typically requires that any user or system benefiting from the features of the service must be appropriately licensed. Therefore, purchasing a single license, performing an automated scan and classification of documents, and then deactivating the service would not align with the licensing terms, as the benefit derived from the scan and classification would extend beyond the period during which the license is active. For further clarification, Microsoft's licensing agreements generally mandate ongoing licensing for any service from which the organization continues to derive benefits. This principle applies to scenarios like automated scanning and classification, which would involve ongoing use of information processed by the service. It is advisable to consult with a Microsoft licensing expert or reseller to ensure compliance with the licensing terms relevant to your specific scenario. : This question below came in from Atea. I put it in Barry's chat and got the answer below, can someone please double check this? TY, - Will Chat with Directions on Microsoft - You: Q: Hello, I would like to confirm a licensing question. If a customer purchases Information Protection and Governance, which includes the Purview Information Protection scanner or client, is it permissible from a licensing perspective to purchase a single license, perform an automated scan and classification of documents, and then deactivate the service? In other words, this would be a one-time action. Is this acceptable under the licensing terms? A: The licensing terms for Microsoft Information Protection and Governance do not permit such use. Licensing for cloud services such as Purview Information Protection and Governance typically requires that any user or system benefiting from the features of the service must be appropriately licensed. Therefore, purchasing a single license, performing an automated scan and classification of documents, and then deactivating the service would not align with the licensing terms, as the benefit derived from the scan and classification would extend beyond the period during which the license is active. For further clarification, Microsoft's licensing agreements generally mandate ongoing licensing for any service from which the organization continues to derive benefits. This principle applies to scenarios like automated scanning and classification, which would involve ongoing use of information processed by the service. It is advisable to consult with a Microsoft licensing expert or reseller to ensure compliance with the licensing terms relevant to your specific scenario. References: - doc1: Purview_Information_Protection_&_Governance_Roadmap - doc2: Purview_Information_Protection_and_Governance - doc3: Microsoft_365_E3_vs._E5__Information_Protection - doc4: Controlling_Azure_Information_Protection_Keys - doc5: Azure_Information_Protection_and_Mobile_Devices

Questions in Query: Andy/Rob - FYI I already told her this is more of an Expert Desk type question and send her the link to the PowerBI kit, we are working on a larger deal with Atea, and this will help build more value with diverse stakeholders w/in the org. Can your team help me with this? There is a lot of background, but the questions are not too many ? As I summarize this, we are interested in the following costs: • What will a license to freely disseminate Power BI graphs in our organization cost? • Are there costs related to using a shared workspace? And are there limitations to sharing data from a workspace? • Is it other than workspace that is the recommended storage area, Teams doesn't seem to work very well? • So, I see both environments will sniff more at datalakes and secure warehouses, but it will probably be too unspecified to say anything special about costs... FROM THE ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT: We have many wishes when it comes to the use of Power BI and first and foremost it is a fantastic tool to visualize data in a simple and easy-to-understand way. But at the same time it is a complex work and setting up the reports and using the basic data in a good way. Our need is first and foremost to be able to make reports available to our managers / employees through both Teams and the web in such a way that they get an expanded insight into professional topics. This could be both theme-based but also insight from systems we use. We want to build up a structure in Workspace that is based on topics / subjects so that this is clear and easier to manage and this is the necessary way to go. In that sense, there is no connection between Teams and Workspace. When creating reports in Power BI, they must be published in order to be shared. If it is not possible to create a workspace for the topic / subject, all a store, will remain on the personal workspace and must be shared from there. It goes without saying that this quickly becomes chaotic. The second area we want to use Power BI for is exploring data. There are endless amounts of data available both internally and externally. We want to move towards and make greater use of data as a basis for our decisions. Then we need to explore the data and use that as a basis. Here, Power BI is a great and easy opportunity for access. Power BI can both be used in a simple way linked to a single static data source but not least, it can be connected to complex and multiple data sources that are dynamic and constantly provide us with new data. We want to take advantage of both of these opportunities. What we have faced in terms of challenges so far is mostly on the licensing front. That is, in order to use the licenses, users must have the right access, regardless of where the reports are shared. Another important factor in this context is the collection of data. A lot can be brought directly into PBI, but the processing and processing of the data can quickly become complex. Then it would probably be appropriate to collect data in theme sheets so that we could retrieve the data directly from there without going the way of connecting to data sources in each individual report. Very nice that you are addressing this as we believe that this will be an area with great development and a greater need in the organization in the future. FROM SOCIETY AND INNOVATION: The task of the analysis group in Strategy is to further develop the knowledge base that will be used in work on social development. • The sources are currently mostly public sources such as Statistics Norway. Statistics Norway has a nice API where we get data from. • From some other public sources (NIPH etc.) it unfortunately has to be downloaded manually. • The data is currently stored in Excel files in a common Teams/ SharePoint folder. • Using PowerBI (and Excel), reports are saved based on this data. Regarding further use of PowerBI: • Our desire is to publish the data we have access to both internally and externally, where the PowerBI solution is preferred. • We have realized that a Teams platform is not the best solution when it is going to be published in the wider world. • In any case, we currently have no way of publishing the report except for export via PowerPoint. Here we need guidance. • There is a desire to connect to internal professional systems and have tools to be able to de-identify and process these data. • Therefore, there is the question: Might it be appropriate to build a database/ sea/warehouse in Azure?

: As discussed, we are facing risk related to capacity constraints with our Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) environment, which supports our store operations. Microsoft has advised that the only way to guarantee capacity for our preferred AVD VM size is to provision and operate AVD hosts 24x7 or to utilize On-Demand Capacity Reservations for all our AVD hosts. This approach conflicts with our planned and budgeted operating model, which relies on Nerdio’s auto-scale features to run AVD hosts only during store business hours. Operating AVD hosts continuously undermines the ROI, intended cost efficiencies, and value proposition of Nerdio, which we have already purchased. : ** from WG: former EA customer, current member show them some love ** Question: Hi Will: I have an odd question. Have you or any of the analysts ever heard of the Enterprise Agreement referred to as an “open catalog” whereby any user registered for access to current or prior (expired) enrollments under Volume Licensing would have access to any and all software under all enrollments, though their keys may be limited to a specific contract – which doesn’t do much good when so many volume licensing software programs don’t require keys. I know that what I setup a user in Volume Licensing on the Microsoft 365 Admin Center, I assign them to a specific contract and then specific roles tied to each particular contract – tiered security. I am now being informed that all of that is for nought as access to any contract is enough to provide the “keys to the kingdom” from a licensing perspective. I’ve been doing this for almost 20 years – this is NOT how I remember this working. And I am wondering if your analysts have heard anything about this? Thanks, Shannon Shannon T. Cocheo IT Software License Management | Duke Energy shannon.cocheo@duke-energy.com | o: 704.382.6659 | c: 704.609.7415 Copilot AI Reply: Here’s how I’d unpack this for the customer — clearly, calmly, and with the authority of someone who actually understands how Microsoft licensing systems *really* behave. --- # 🧠 **Short Answer** No — an Enterprise Agreement is **not** an “open catalog,” and simply having access to *one* enrollment does **not** grant entitlement to *all* software across *all* enrollments. What *is* true is that the **Volume Licensing Service Center (VLSC)** has some legacy behaviors that *look* broader than they actually are — but they do **not** override contract‑specific entitlements. Your customer’s instincts are correct: **This is not how licensing entitlement works, and it’s not how it has ever worked.** --- # 🧩 **Where the Confusion Comes From** There are two systems at play: ### **1. VLSC (legacy)** - Historically allowed users to *see* multiple agreements if they were associated with the same organization or domain. - The UI sometimes exposed **download catalogs** that looked broad or generic. - But **visibility ≠ entitlement**. - Entitlement is always tied to the specific enrollment and the specific SKUs purchased. ### **2. Microsoft 365 Admin Center (modern)** - Much more granular. - You assign users to **specific contracts** and **specific roles**. - This is the correct and current model. - Nothing in the modern system converts an EA into an “open catalog.” --- # 🔐 **Key Principle** ### **Entitlement is contract‑bound. Access is user‑bound. These are not the same thing.** Even if a user can *see* software downloads in VLSC, they are **not entitled** to deploy or use software outside the SKUs purchased under their specific enrollment. This is why: - True‑ups - License verifications - SAM engagements - Audit defense all rely on **contract SKUs**, not what a user can see in VLSC. --- # 🛑 **What is *not* true** The claim that: > “Access to any contract gives you the keys to the kingdom.” is **incorrect**. A user with access to one enrollment: - cannot legally deploy software from another enrollment - is not granted rights to expired agreements - does not inherit entitlements from unrelated contracts - does not gain rights to products not purchased Even if the download center *shows* the bits. --- # 🧠 **Why this misconception pops up** There are three common reasons: ### **1. VLSC’s download catalog is not entitlement‑aware** It shows software families broadly (e.g., Windows Server, SQL Server), even if the customer only owns specific versions or editions. ### **2. Many Microsoft products don’t require keys** This makes people think “If I can download it, I must be entitled.” Not true — entitlement is contractual, not technical. ### **3. Expired agreements remain visible** VLSC keeps historical agreements for record‑keeping. Visibility ≠ rights. --- # 🧭 **How to respond to the customer (copy/paste ready)** **“You’re correct — an Enterprise Agreement is not an ‘open catalog.’ Even if VLSC shows a broad set of downloads, entitlement is always tied to the specific enrollment and the specific SKUs purchased. Access to one contract does not grant rights to software under other contracts, current or expired. The modern Microsoft 365 Admin Center enforces contract‑specific roles and permissions exactly as you’ve been doing. VLSC’s legacy behavior can create the appearance of broader access, but it does not override licensing rights or create cross‑contract entitlements.”** --- # 🎯 **If you want, I can also draft:** - a **formal analyst‑style answer** you can send back to the customer - a **more technical explanation** of how VLSC and M365 Admin Center differ - a **risk‑mitigation statement** for audit‑sensitive customers Just tell me the tone you want.
